regime_classification.2026-04-09:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"Headline CPI at 3.21% masks a dangerous internal rotation: food inflation jumped to 3.47% from 2.13% in a single month while fuel deflation of -3.78% is doing the statistical heavy lifting. regime_classification.2026-04-09:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — 81st percentile over a decade — with credit growing at 13.8% against deposit growth of 10.8%, a 300bps divergence that has widened for consecutive months. regime_classification.2026-04-09:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real rate of approximately 200bps — restrictive enough to justify a cut, but Brent at $98 and food inflation accelerating to 3.47% give the MPC legitimate cover to wait. regime_classification.2026-04-09:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% YoY — up sharply from 4.3% — is the most encouraging number in today's dataset, but core sector at 2.3% (cement, steel, electricity, coal) directly contradicts it: you cannot have genuine capex acceleration without core sector throughput. Real estate launches at 126,265 units — 0th percentile over a decade, an all-time low — despite an affordability index improving to 0.61 and home loan rates falling to 8% from 9.1%, which suggests developers are not convinced demand is durable enough to commit capital. Munger's inversion: if private capex were genuinely accelerating, GST collections on capex-intensive sectors would be outpacing the headline ₹2,00,640 crore — they are not.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST at ₹2,00,640 crore crossing the ₹2 lakh crore threshold is structurally significant, but the Mishra question is whether this reflects genuine demand expansion or formalization reclassifying existing informal spend — two-wheeler sales at 28.68% YoY and CV sales at 28.89% suggest rural and freight demand is genuinely strong, not just a base effect. Passenger vehicle sales at 26.1% YoY confirm urban discretionary is holding, but airline passenger traffic at the 7th percentile and e-commerce GMV growth decelerating from 25% to 12.4% suggest the upper-middle consumer is pulling back. Retail mall vacancy rising to 5.7% from 5.1% while e-commerce decelerates simultaneously is the most underappreciated signal: neither physical nor digital retail is accelerating — consumption breadth is narrower than the headline implies.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"PMI Composite at 56.5 flatters to deceive: Manufacturing PMI fell to 53.9 from 56.9, core sector decelerated to 2.3% from 4.7%, and airline passenger traffic dropped 9% to a 18-month low — none of which is consistent with a 7.8% economy firing on all cylinders. Invert the GDP print: the 170bps gap between the RBI's own forecast of 6.9% and the reported 7.8% demands a deflator explanation, not a celebration. What breaks this narrative is simple — if IIP capital goods momentum (up 12.5% this month but from a 4.3% prior base) doesn't sustain, the growth story reverts to its GVA skeleton, which is structurally closer to 6.5%.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"Headline CPI at 3.21% is benign, but the composition is a slow-motion trap: food inflation has jumped to 3.47% from 2.13% in a single print, FAO Food Index is at 128.5 (a 2.5% MoM rise), and Brent at $96.44 means the fuel deflation buffer of -3.78% is arithmetically finite — one administered price revision ends it. Core CPI is sticky at 3.4%, unchanged for two consecutive months, which tells you demand-side pricing power hasn't broken despite the rate cycle. Munger's inversion: if RBI cuts into a food-inflation re-acceleration while Brent adds $15bn to the import bill for every $10 move, the second-order effect is a CPI overshoot toward 5% by Q3 FY27 — precisely when the rate cut transmission would be peaking.","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — the 81st percentile of its 10-year range — is the single most important number in today's dataset, and it is moving in the wrong direction from 82.5%. NBFC credit growth has already decelerated from 17% to 13% YoY, the classic early signal of wholesale funding stress; corporate bond issuance at ₹1.12 lakh crore sits at the 7th percentile — a decade low — meaning corporates are not accessing bond markets as a relief valve, they are rationing debt entirely. Historically, the last time India's CD ratio sustained above 80% during a rate-cut cycle was Q2 FY14; within four quarters, the taper tantrum forced 75bps of emergency RBI hikes — the setup today rhymes, except the external trigger this time is Brent, not Fed tapering.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real policy rate of approximately +200bps — restrictive by India's historical standards, where neutral real rates have averaged 100-150bps. RBI's own inflation forecast has been revised up to 4.6% from 4.2%, which compresses the cutting room: a 25bps cut would still leave real rates above neutral, but the CD ratio at 83% means any rate cut that stimulates credit demand without a corresponding deposit response risks a macro-prudential response — higher risk weights on unsecured lending, as seen in November 2023. Home loan rates falling to 8% from 9.1% have already improved the affordability index to 0.61 from 0.46 — the transmission is working in housing; the question is whether the RBI can hold this line if food inflation prints above 4% in May.","badge_label":"Rate Pause","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at +12.5% YoY looks strong until you note it follows a +4.3% prior print — the base effect is doing most of the work, not a genuine acceleration in investment orders. Core sector at 2.3% — covering steel, cement, electricity, and coal — is the most reliable capex proxy India has, and at an 18-month low, it contradicts the capital goods IIP entirely; cement dispatch and steel production don't lie about construction activity the way a single-month IIP print can. Capacity utilisation data is unavailable today, but auto and cement order book commentary points to plateauing utilisation — and Munger's question is the right one: if utilisation isn't above 75-80%, the incentive for private greenfield capex simply doesn't exist, regardless of what the budget allocates.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST collections at ₹2.006 lakh crore are a record, but Mishra's lens demands decomposition: at this level, a meaningful share of the YoY gain reflects formalization reclassification — informal-to-formal migration — rather than net new demand creation, particularly as e-commerce GMV growth has halved from 25% to 12.4% YoY, the 15th percentile of its range. Vehicle sales tell a bifurcated story: PV at +26.1%, two-wheelers at +28.68%, and CVs at +28.89% all signal broad-based demand — but airline passenger traffic at 35.08 lakh, down 9% and at a 7th-percentile low, suggests urban discretionary spending is softening at the margin. Invert the GST record: if consumption were genuinely broad-based, e-commerce GMV and airline traffic — the two most real-time consumption proxies — would not both be printing multi-year lows simultaneously.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}

executive_summary.2026-04-08:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

GDP 7.8% with PMI Composite at 57 puts India in unambiguous expansion — 3 of 6 macro dimensions are in growth mode simultaneously. GST collections at ₹2,00,064 Cr YoY alongside PV sales growth of 10.5% confirm demand is broad-based, not sector-specific. CPI at 3.21% with fuel deflation at -3.78% gives RBI room to hold the repo at 5.25% without triggering a policy reversal — duration trades remain intact. Credit growth at 14.3% against deposit growth of 10.9% — CD ratio now 83.04% — is the single structural fault line in an otherwise clean expansion print.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:2:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

S&P 500 at 6,779 (+2.5%) and Nasdaq at 22,653 (+3.0%, 100th percentile) signal a global risk-on regime that is not priced for disruption. Brent at $94.98/bbl — down 13% from $109.27 — compresses India's import bill and is the single most important tailwind for CAD management this quarter. Copper at $5.766/lb (95th percentile, 10-year range) and iron ore surging to $161.91/t from $108.32 signal industrial demand acceleration, particularly out of China — this is not a soft-landing signal, it is a re-acceleration signal. DXY at 98.84 and US 10Y at 4.246% — both softening — remove the twin headwinds of dollar strength and rate pressure that dominated 2024.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:3:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":3,"para_label":"Liquidity Conditions","para_html":"

SIP AUM at ₹16.64 Tr on 9.92 Cr accounts is structural bedrock — a -3.7% MoM dip in SIP inflows to ₹29,845 Cr is noise against 15% YoY growth. Equity MF net inflows accelerated to ₹25,978 Cr (prev: ₹24,029 Cr), and NFO collections exploded to ₹3,955 Cr from ₹806 Cr — DII buying is not slowing, it is rotating into new paper. India VIX collapsing 20.25% to 19.70 from 24.7 — concurrent with US VIX falling to 21.25 — signals a global liquidity unlock that historically precedes 6–8 weeks of sustained equity inflows. FII positioning remains the swing variable; INR stability at 92.59 against a weakening DXY (98.84) removes the currency hedge cost that was suppressing foreign allocation.

"}

executive_summary.2026-04-09:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

7.8% GDP growth with PMI composite at 56.5 is the headline; the subtext is a growth regime that is decelerating on every sub-indicator that matters — manufacturing PMI fell 300bps in a month to 53.9, core sector output dropped to 2.3% from 4.7%, and the RBI's own forecast of 6.9% sits 90bps below the statistical agency's print. GST at ₹2,00,640 crore — the first ₹2 lakh crore month — is real, but two-wheeler sales at 28.68% and CV sales at 28.89% YoY are doing more analytical work than the GST headline: rural and freight demand is genuinely recovering, not just being formalized. IIP capital goods at 12.5% would be unambiguously positive if core sector and cement dispatches corroborated it — they do not, which makes this a data point to verify, not celebrate. India's growth is real but narrowing: the formal economy is expanding, the informal economy is stabilizing, and the capex cycle is more promise than proof.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:2:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

US GDP SAAR at 0.7% — down from 4.4%, a 370bps deceleration in a single quarter — is the most consequential global data point this week, yet S&P 500 at the 96th percentile and Nasdaq at an all-time high suggest equity markets have decided this is a soft patch, not a stall. US core PCE at 3.1%, ticking up from 3.0%, with Fed funds at 3.625% and the balance sheet expanding to $6,675bn, means the Fed is simultaneously easing liquidity while inflation re-accelerates — a combination that historically resolves with either a policy mistake or a growth shock. Brent at $98.06 and WTI at $99.67 — with iron ore surging 50% in a month to $161.91/tonne — suggest commodity markets are pricing a China demand revival that China's own GDP at 4.5% (down from 5%) does not yet justify. Global PMI composite at 51 — barely above contraction — with the US at 50.3 and Europe at sub-1% GDP growth means India's 7.8% is an outlier that will attract capital only if the INR holds.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:3:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":3,"para_label":"Liquidity Conditions","para_html":"

FII equity net flows swung ₹22,729 crore negative in a single month — from +₹22,615 crore to -₹114 crore — while DII support at +₹128 crore is at the 22nd percentile, meaning domestic institutions are not absorbing the FII exit with conviction. SIP inflows at ₹29,845 crore are declining from ₹31,002 crore, and SIP YoY growth has decelerated from 17% to 15% — the retail liquidity engine that cushioned every FII exit since 2022 is running at lower pressure. CD ratio at 83.04% — with credit at 13.8% and deposits at 10.8% — means the banking system's internal liquidity is being consumed faster than it is being replenished, forcing corporate bond issuance to ₹1,12,000 crore as a pressure valve. If RBI cuts 50bps as markets expect, the India-US rate differential compresses to 140bps at the precise moment FII outflows are accelerating — the INR at the 84th percentile has no margin for error.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:4:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":4,"para_label":"Equity + Real Estate Implications","para_html":"

Nifty at 23,775 — 76th percentile — with Bank Nifty at the 90th percentile and India VIX rising to 20.4 from 19.7 creates a specific risk: banks are the most expensive segment of a market facing the most acute structural stress — CD ratio at 83%, NBFC deceleration, and potential macro-prudential tightening on unsecured lending. Real estate presents the sharpest internal contradiction in today's dataset: affordability at a decade-best 0.61, home loan rates at 8% (down from 9.1%), and ₹5,50,000 crore in disbursements — yet launches at an all-time low 0th percentile and sales declining to 1,01,675 units from 1,08,970, with unsold inventory at 6,00,000 units. Office absorption at 29.9 mn sqft with vacancy compressing 330bps to 13.9% is the one clean positive — Bengaluru rents up 8.4% to ₹102/sqft — but Nifty IT flat YoY means the equity market is not pricing the physical demand that office REITs are experiencing. Embassy REIT at ₹437 and Mindspace at ₹450 — both near 79th percentile — are the better expression of the office thesis than IT equities.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:5:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Ranked by probability × impact: first, Brent sustaining above $98 — every $10 adds ~$15bn to India's import bill — CAD widens 40bps — INR depreciates 2-3% — imported food inflation feeds CPI with a two-quarter lag, directly threatening the rate-cut narrative and the INR stability that FII re-entry requires. Second, CD ratio breaching 84% — the level at which RBI has historically intervened with macro-prudential measures on risk weights — triggering NBFC funding costs to spike, credit growth to decelerate sharply, and consumption-linked equities to re-rate lower. Third, US core PCE at 3.1% and rising with GDP at 0.7% — stagflation is not the base case but it is now a 25% probability scenario — DXY strengthening from 98.9 would accelerate FII outflows from EM including India at the worst possible moment. Fourth, iron ore at $161.91 — a 50% monthly surge — if sustained, steel input costs rise, infrastructure project IRRs compress, and the capex cycle that IIP capital goods at 12.5% is supposed to signal gets deferred. Watch Friday's RBI liquidity data and weekly deposit growth figures — if the credit-deposit gap widens beyond 300bps for a third consecutive month, the bear scenario probability doubles.

"}

Fed funds at 3.625% with US core PCE at 3.1% (up from 3.0%) means the Fed's real rate is barely positive — insufficient to declare victory, insufficient to cut — a policy paralysis that keeps the dollar range-bound with DXY at 98.8. Iron ore's 50% surge to $161.91 on China stimulus expectations, alongside Hang Seng up 2.5% and China CPI recovering to 1.3% from 0.2%, suggests Beijing's reflation is gaining traction — which is simultaneously good for India's export demand and bad for India's commodity import bill. CD ratio at 83.04% — the 81st percentile of its decade range — is the structural constraint that no rate cut can solve: deposit growth at 10.8% is simply not keeping pace with credit demand at 13.8%, and the gap is widening. FII equity net flows swung from +₹22,615 crore to -₹114 crore — a ₹22,729 crore reversal in a single period — with INR stability at 92.59 maintained only by visible RBI intervention, which depletes the reserve buffer that is India's CAD shock absorber. SIP inflows at ₹29,845 crore are declining from ₹31,002 crore, YoY growth decelerating to 15% from 17% — domestic equity liquidity is still positive but the marginal momentum is fading precisely when FII support has evaporated. Corporate bond issuance at the 7th percentile of its decade range signals that corporates are not accessing capital markets as a deposit-gap relief valve — they are rationing debt, which is a leading indicator of capex compression in 2-3 quarters.

Office absorption at 29.9 mn sqft with vacancy collapsing to 13.9% from 17.2% and Bengaluru rents up 9% to ₹102/sqft is the most underappreciated positive in today's dataset — commercial real estate is tightening faster than equity markets have priced. Residential real estate shows a divergence: home loan rates falling to 8% have lifted the affordability index to 0.61, but sales units are down to 101,675 from 108,970 while unsold inventory has risen to 600,000 units — demand is rate-sensitive but supply is outrunning absorption. Gold in INR terms at ₹2,000/gram — an all-time high — is the market's verdict on INR trajectory and real return expectations: when gold is at the 84th percentile in USD and the 0th percentile (all-time high) in INR, the currency is doing the heavy lifting of the inflation story.

executive_summary.2026-04-10:5:
{"run_date":"2026-04-10","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Ranked by probability × impact: first, Brent sustaining above $95 — every $10 adds $15bn to India's import bill — CAD widens 40bps — INR depreciates 2-3% — imported inflation feeds CPI with a 2-quarter lag, directly threatening the RBI's 4.6% inflation forecast and closing the rate-cut window entirely. Second, CD ratio breaching 84% — the level at which RBI macro-prudential action (risk weight hikes on unsecured and NBFC lending) becomes near-certain — triggering NBFC credit growth below 10% and a consumption credit squeeze that would expose the fragility beneath the GST record. Third, food inflation printing above 4% in May on the back of FAO Food Index at 128.5 and a below-normal pre-monsoon — a combination that forces RBI to hold rates through H1 FY27 even as growth decelerates toward the RBI's own 6.9% forecast. Fourth, FII outflow acceleration if US recession fears deepen — DXY at 98.8 is benign, but a US hard landing would reprice EM risk premia and test RBI's FX intervention capacity against a $96 oil import bill simultaneously. Watch Friday's RBI liquidity operations and the April CPI print — if food inflation exceeds 3.8%, the rate-cut consensus collapses and Bank Nifty's 90th-percentile valuation becomes the most exposed trade in the book.

"}
